content: Tags help organize feature flags and optimize SDK performance. Setting tags on flags allows SDKs to fetch only the flags they need, reducing payload size and improving evaluation speed. Best practices: Use consistent naming (e.g., team-name, feature-area, release-version). Ensure every flag has at least one tag.

Feature flag lifecycle management is essential for keeping your codebase clean. Release flags should be archived after 100% rollout. Flags unused for 7+ days should be reviewed. Kill Switch flags can be kept permanently. Regular cleanup prevents flag debt and improves system performance.

Segments allow you to group users for reusable targeting across multiple flags. Instead of duplicating targeting rules, create a Segment (e.g., 'Premium Users', 'Beta Testers'). Segments support CSV bulk upload for managing large user sets. Changes to a segment automatically apply to all flags using it.

Progressive Rollout enables gradual feature releases by incrementing the percentage of users who see a feature over time. Use Manual Schedule for custom increments or Template Schedule for predefined patterns (e.g., 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100%). If issues are detected, the rollout can be paused or rolled back.

Flag Triggers allow external systems to control feature flags via webhook URLs. Use cases include: CI/CD pipeline integration for automatic flag enablement after deployment, incident response automation to disable features quickly, and external monitoring integration. Each trigger generates a unique URL that can be called with a POST request.

Prerequisite Rules allow you to create dependencies between feature flags. A flag with prerequisites will only be evaluated when all prerequisite flags meet their specified conditions. This is useful for microservices coordination and staged feature releases where features depend on each other.

The Debugger tool allows you to test how feature flags evaluate for specific users. Enter a user ID and attributes to see which variation would be served and why. This is essential for troubleshooting targeting rules and verifying flag behavior before production deployment.

Experiments in Bucketeer use Bayesian statistical analysis for A/B testing. Create a Goal to define what you want to measure, then set up an Experiment linking a feature flag to the goal. The system automatically calculates statistical significance and provides actionable results with conversion rates and confidence intervals.
